And recent research shows that bad sleep can increase the risks of multiple health conditions. How harmful is it to get insufficient or irregular sleep? and given how common it is, can it be improved? One size does not fit all. And there have been some somewhat irresponsible sort of statements saying, you know, if you don't get eight hours of sleep, you're going to die. Well, that's just nonsense. I mean, the healthy range for sleep from the National Sleep Foundation, which I'm a member of that board, is somewhere between six hours and it may be up to ten and a half, even 11 hours for some people. The average, true, is about seven to eight hours, but there's lots of variation between individuals.",

I left the academic world and ended up in a health screening clinic where I was measuring the health of city professionals. I love that word that Russell's used, but not just at night, but throughout the day, oscillating. All of the physiological processes in the body, they oscillate in a kind of sinusoidal up-and-down rhythm. And I think the way we're living our lives, we've become very restless. And as we get older, if you look at shift workers, the impact, you know, disease risk in shift workers is well documented, particularly as they get older, past the age of 35, 40, disease risk increases.       "transcript": "We produced a model a few years ago suggesting that, yes, at the core, there's an overlap between the fundamental mechanisms of the brain that are generating normal sleep and those that are generating normal mental health. But the key thing is the poor sleep, because of its impact on our cognition, our emotional responses and our physiological responses, can make the mental health condition worse. And poor mental health can feed back and make the sleep worse. So we did some experiments a few years ago on individuals who were on the psychosis spectrum. This is led by Dan Freeman, whereby we were able to partially stabilise sleep. And what was so wonderful is that you were able to reduce the levels of paranoia and the hallucinatory experiences. So there's an absolute link between poor sleep and levels of mental health severity. Russell, what about mental degeneration? Because there has been talk of a link to dementia. I mean, there's been a long sort of correlation that poor sleep in the middle years is associated as a risk factor for dementia in the later years. And there's never been a really satisfactory explanation for that until recently. And there's been a recently discovered system within the brain called the glymphatic system, which clears out toxins and misfolded proteins, one of which is beta amyloid. and the build-up of beta-amyloid, which is like chewing gum, sticking in cells and making them not work very well, and a build-up of beta-amyloid has been associated, of course, with dementia and Alzheimer's. Now, the glymphatic system is turned on whilst we sleep and actually is clearing out some of those misfolded proteins and other toxins.       "transcript": "I want to turn, if I can, to the impact of, I guess, both the lifestyles people lead and also where they live in terms of how much daylight they see. It's really about the impact, I guess, of less daylight upon people and whether it changes how much sleep they need or that they feel they need. Russell, has there been research into this? Yes, quite a bit. We've done some, and there are many labs around the world. So basically, what the circadian system needs to be of any use at all is to set the internal clock to the external world. And the most important daily signal is the light-dark cycle. And so morning exposure to light especially is really important in setting the clock. Noreen, how much have you worked with night shift workers and the impact that their work has on their sleep patterns and their health? Yes. Quite a considerable amount, actually. I've done quite a bit of work in the past with police services, other emergency services, hospital workers. Yeah, helping them to manage those shifts and to manage their rest when they're working shifts and when they come off shifts. It's a really important consideration because there are so many health implications of working shifts and missing out on those crucial kind of hours of sleep that they should be getting at night. I mean, is it as unhealthy as we are now led to believe? The levels of light and dark and the way that impacts the health, it impacts our physiology and night shift workers. Shift workers are very prone to not just physical ailments, they can be prone to mental health, emotional and even the social implications. I think the divorce rate. And what's interesting, I think, is that the assumption by employers is that, oh, people will adapt to the night shift work. And they don't. And the reason they don't is because of light. So they're under relatively dim light in the workplace at night, then will be exposed to natural light during the day. And the clock will always defer to the brighter light signal as being daytime, which it is. So in a sense, what you're doing is having to override this entire biology saying you should be asleep. And one of the problems, of course, is that you override it by activating the stress axis. And as we know, sustained stress activation distorts our physiology. One aspect that gets hit is our immune system. So night shift workers are more vulnerable to infection and indeed cancer. So the World Health Organization now has designated night shift work as a probable carcinogen because of the higher rates of colorectal cancer, breast cancer, that have been documented in night shift nurses.",

I think that's very important. So these are the five things that I recommend to my clients with the clients, patients and any aversion of this to schoolchildren as well and athletes, all of them. These five things, what they do, 10 to 14 days of doing these things, I have noticed they start to reset the nervous system from this highly activated stress side to this more restful sleep accessing part of the nervous system. OK, have I sold it? No, I'm gasping for it now. I can't wait. OK, number one. That's good. I'm gasping for it now, I can't wait. You're smiling, you're gasping. Okay. Eat breakfast in the morning. Don't skip breakfast. So eat within about 30 to 45 minutes of rising. with Big thumbs up from Russell. OK, thank you. So number two, don't use caffeine as a substitute for food. Bye-bye. I don't know. Hmm. Eat first, then have your coffee. That's what I did this morning. Ideally, no more than one or two a day. Ideally, no caffeine after midday, I would say. Number three, make sure you're well hydrated. Number four, get to bed earlier. So I recommend that people start preparing to go to bed around 9.30, 10ish. I don't mean get into bed and be asleep, but get into your lovely haven of a bedroom and be restful. You could be reading a book. You could be meditating. You could be journaling. That's what I want. video. Gracias. I don't know. This is about sort of advising your body that it is bedtime. Yes, and finding that... Yes. And finding that sweet spot for restfulness, which will enable you to kind of access then those deeper phases of restoration. Number five. Yeah. Stop flooding the brain with the wrong kind of light at the end of the day. So Russell talked about getting light in the morning, the light shower in the morning. But similarly, we need to pay attention to how we're misusing light at the end of the day. What's the wrong kind of light? We'll be right back. It's the doom scrolling and all the rest of it. of honor. all the right And that's actively different from reading a book. Well, actually, the initial study suggested that it was. It is. It is. When we're reading a book, sorry, the eyes, when you're reading a paper book, your eyes are moving almost in a hypnotic way. Gracias. Yeah. Hmm. left to right often when people are reading on a screen and even if it's one of those light corrected screens you know they're sort of reading almost in a question mark you know the eyes start to speed up and speed you know speed reading but as as russell said it's that that doom scrolling and what we put into our minds before we go to bed has an impact on where we we reside in the nervous system and at the end of the day we sleep when the hunter-gatherer feels safe the hunter gatherer sleeps if you're doom scrolling if you're looking at the news if you're looking at the dating sites telling you that no one loves you if you're looking at share prices if you're looking at all that your emails and what's going to hit you the next day that doesn't make you feel safe up. We will leave it there. Russell, Lorena, thank you so, so much for both your analysis of what has been going wrong and your suggestions of how things might go right. Thank you very much indeed. Great pleasure. Thank you. Beijo. Great pleasure. Thank you very much. Great pleasure.",

think_blocks:
The user wants me to segment the video based on transitions in the conversation or narrative structure.

1.  **Analyze the video:**
    *   **00:00 - 00:58:** Introduction by the host, Jonny Dymond. He introduces the topic of sleep, the guests (Russell Foster and Dr. Nerina Ramlakhan), and sets the stage.
    *   **00:58 - 01:19:** The host asks the guests how they slept. This is a brief icebreaker.
    *   **01:19 - 02:46:** The host asks Russell Foster to explain circadian rhythms and the recommended amount of sleep. Russell explains the concept and the variability in sleep needs.
    *   **02:46 - 04:49:** The host asks Nerina about her experience helping people sleep and the impact of modern life's speed on sleep. Nerina discusses the loss of the ability to slow down and oscillate.
    *   **04:49 - 06:05:** The host asks how to tell if you're not getting enough sleep. Nerina advises listening to your body rather than relying on apps.
    *   **06:05 - 07:38:** The host asks Russell about waking up in the middle of the night. Russell explains that it's normal and discusses biphasic/polyphasic sleep.
    *   **07:38 - 09:25:** The host asks about the long-term health effects of bad sleep. Nerina discusses disease risks, and Russell talks about the link to mental health and the glymphatic system.
    *   **09:25 - 11:24:** The host asks about the impact of daylight and shift work. Russell explains the importance of morning light, and Nerina discusses the health implications for shift workers.
    *   **11:24 - 14:14:** The host asks for solutions and top tips. Russell suggests morning light and separate bedrooms if needed. Nerina lists her five tips: breakfast, caffeine, hydration, bedtime routine, and avoiding blue light.
    *   **14:14 - 15:42:** The host wraps up the show, thanking the guests and the audience.

2.  **Identify segmentation points:**
    *   **00:00 - 00:58:** Introduction.
    *   **00:58 - 01:19:** Icebreaker about how guests slept.
    *   **01:19 - 02:46:** Explanation of circadian rhythms and sleep duration.
    *   **02:46 - 04:49:** Impact of modern life's speed on sleep.
    *   **04:49 - 06:05:** How to tell if you're not getting enough sleep.
    *   **06:05 - 07:38:** Waking up in the middle of the night.
    *   **07:38 - 09:25:** Long-term health effects (physical and mental).
    *   **09:25 - 11:24:** Impact of daylight and shift work.
    *   **11:24 - 14:14:** Solutions and top tips for better sleep.
    *   **14:14 - 15:42:** Conclusion.

3.  **Refine segments:**
    *   Segment 1: 00:00 - 00:58 (Intro)
    *   Segment 2: 00:58 - 01:19 (Icebreaker)
    *   Segment 3: 01:19 - 02:46 (Circadian rhythms & duration)
    *   Segment 4: 02:46 - 04:49 (Modern life's speed)
    *   Segment 5: 04:49 - 06:05 (Recognizing sleep deprivation)
    *   Segment 6: 06:05 - 07:38 (Waking up at night)
    *   Segment 7: 07:38 - 09:25 (Health effects)
    *   Segment 8: 09:25 - 11:24 (Daylight & shift work)
    *   Segment 9: 11:24 - 14:14 (Solutions & tips)
    *   Segment 10: 14:14 - 15:42 (Outro)

    Wait, the prompt asks to segment at the exact point where the conversation transitions to a new distinct subtopic or main question. I can combine some of the shorter segments if they flow together well, but the prompt implies distinct subtopics. The identified points seem distinct enough.

    Let's check the timestamps again.
    - 00:58: Host asks "Let me start with the most important question of the day. Noreena, how did you sleep?" -> New topic.
    - 01:19: Host asks "Russell, you specialise in circadian rhythms... What are they?" -> New topic.
    - 02:46: Host asks "Serena, you have spent, what, three decades helping people to get better sleep." -> New topic.
    - 04:49: Host asks "how can you tell when you're not getting enough sleep?" -> New topic.
    - 06:05: Host asks "Russell, I want to ask you about... Whether or not sleep as a block is important." -> New topic.
    - 07:38: Host asks "Part of that anxiety is that people don't want to be exhausted during the day... Are they right?" -> New topic.
    - 09:25: Host asks "I want to turn, if I can, to the impact of, I guess, both the lifestyles people lead and also where they live in terms of how much daylight they see." -> New topic.
    - 11:24: Host asks "I want to talk about solutions very much... Your top tips, if you would, for sleep." -> New topic.
    - 14:14: Host says "We will leave it there." -> Conclusion.

    These transitions are clear.

4.  **Final Polish:** Ensure timestamps are accurate and summaries are concise.

    *   Segment 1: 00:00 - 00:58
    *   Segment 2: 00:58 - 01:19
    *   Segment 3: 01:19 - 02:46
    *   Segment 4: 02:46 - 04:49
    *   Segment 5: 04:49 - 06:05
    *   Segment 6: 06:05 - 07:38
    *   Segment 7: 07:38 - 09:25
    *   Segment 8: 09:25 - 11:24
    *   Segment 9: 11:24 - 14:14
    *   Segment 10: 14:14 - 15:42

    The timestamps look correct. The summaries capture the essence of each segment.

    One small adjustment: Segment 2 is very short (21 seconds). It's a distinct question though. I'll keep it.
    Segment 5 is also relatively short (1 min 16 sec). It's a distinct question. I'll keep it.
